one_line_takeaway: R-CoT embeds an LLM ownership watermark as a trigger-activated redundant self-verification reasoning path trained jointly with the native reasoning path via GRPO/LoRA, achieving near-100% TPR with 0% FPR and retaining >95% TPR even after fine-tuning on up to 80% of new data, though its cross-baseline robustness comparison uses non-matched fine-tuning intensities and heavy fine-tuning still substantially erodes task accuracy on the tested model.
key_finding: Table 1: Llama3.1-8B achieved TPR 99.81% (GSM8K-test) and 99.94% (Math10K), both with FPR 0.00%; Qwen2.5-7B achieved TPR 100.00% on both datasets, also FPR 0.00%. Fidelity: Delta-Acc was 0.28% (Llama3.1-8B/GSM8K-test), -2.57% (Llama3.1-8B/Math10K), -0.85% (Qwen2.5-7B/GSM8K-test), -0.30% (Qwen2.5-7B/Math10K); Delta-NT-Acc was -2.94%, -0.62%, -0.09%, and 0.74% respectively for the same four rows. Robustness to SFT (Table 2, fine-tuning ratio r%): for Llama3.1-8B, TPR declined from 99.81% (r=0) to a minimum of 95.73% at r=80%, while T-Acc dropped from 83.79% to 64.36% at r=100%; for Qwen2.5-7B, TPR stayed above 98% across all r and T-Acc stayed roughly stable (84.6-87.7%). Under input-layer attacks (zero-width-character and homoglyph substitution on the trigger, Fig. 3b), TPR remained above 90% across tested temperature/top-p settings for the Llama3.1-8B model. Comparative Table 3 (paper's own aggregation of self- vs. cited results): R-CoT TPR 99.9% with worst-case |Delta|NT-Acc <=2.94%, vs. Dataset WM TPR 97.4% (~0.5% NT-Acc gap, no reported SFT robustness) and CRMark TPR 96.5% (85.8% retained effectiveness at r=2% mild SFT, 68.3% at r=8% strong SFT); R-CoT retained 99.8% TPR at r=10% (its own 'mild' setting) and 95.7% at r=80% (its own 'strong' setting) -- the paper explicitly flags that the SFT ratios compared across methods are not matched (R-CoT's mild/strong are 10%/80% vs. CRMark's 2%/8%), calling its own comparison 'conservative and metric-aligned' using each method's originally reported numbers rather than a re-run head-to-head.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not the surveyed related work) is R-CoT (Redundant Chain-of-Thought), a reasoning-layer LLM watermarking framework that embeds ownership information as a trigger-activated internal reasoning path rather than as surface-level output text or as CoT content that is treated as watermark payload. The model is trained (via LoRA + GRPO, Group Relative Policy Optimization) with a Dual-trajectory Optimization Mechanism so a 'native' reasoning path (used on non-trigger inputs) and a 'redundant/watermark' reasoning path (used on trigger inputs, instantiated as unnecessary but correctness-preserving self-verification/re-check steps within the <REASON> segment) coexist stably in the same shared parameter space. The trigger is a low-frequency reversed-string token ('thgirypoc'). Watermark verification is black-box: a trigger-containing query with the watermark system prompt is issued, and an operator E(y)=I[Trigger(y)=1 AND Pos(y)=1] checks both that the redundant CoT pattern is present and that it is correctly located inside the <REASON> segment.
threat_model: Two parties, stated explicitly in Section 3.1: (1) Model Owner -- embeds a secret watermark during training, knows the full trigger set and verification procedure, and verifies ownership of a suspected model in a black-box manner; (2) Attacker -- reuses the released model without authorization and attempts to weaken/remove the watermark via post-training modifications (e.g., supervised fine-tuning), with no knowledge of the trigger set or the redundancy design. The paper also separately evaluates 'security against input-layer attacks' -- zero-width character insertion and homoglyph substitution used to corrupt the trigger token at the encoding level while preserving visual appearance.
limitations: The paper's cross-method comparison in Table 3 uses non-matched fine-tuning intensities between R-CoT (10%/80% of data) and CRMark (2%/8% of data), which the authors themselves flag with footnotes but which still complicates any claim of a fair robustness advantage. Robustness/attack evaluation (Fig. 3, input-layer attacks, trigger position/decoding sweep) is reported only for Llama3.1-8B, not replicated for Qwen2.5-7B. Fidelity degradation is non-trivial at the OOD Math10K setting (Delta-Acc -2.57% for Llama3.1-8B) and the SFT robustness experiment shows T-Acc collapsing from 83.79% to 64.36% for Llama3.1-8B at r=100%, i.e. heavy fine-tuning still substantially damages task accuracy even though TPR (watermark detectability) is preserved -- the paper frames this as a watermark-robustness success without fully addressing that the underlying model utility is degraded. The attacker model assumes no knowledge of the trigger or redundancy design; robustness against an adaptive attacker who knows the general R-CoT approach (e.g., systematically stripping verification-like reasoning steps) is not evaluated. Only math-reasoning tasks (GSM8K, Math10K) and two base models are tested; generalization to non-math reasoning domains is not shown.

quantitative_results: SFT robustness, Qwen2.5-7B: TPR stayed >=98% across r=0-100%, T-Acc stayed roughly 84.6-87.7%
Input-layer attack (zero-width/homoglyph on trigger), Llama3.1-8B: TPR remained above 90% across tested temperature/top-p settings

Comparative Table 3: R-CoT TPR 99.9%, worst-case |Delta|NT-Acc <=2.94%, TPR 99.8% at r=10% (mild), 95.7% at r=80% (strong); Dataset WM TPR 97.4%, ~0.5% NT-Acc gap, no reported SFT robustness; CRMark TPR 96.5%, 85.8% effectiveness at r=2% (mild), 68.3% at r=8% (strong)
baselines_compared: Dataset-based SFT watermarking (Qiu et al., 2025) -- cited prior work, not reproduced by this paper, numbers taken from original report
CRMark (Guo et al., 2025b) -- cited prior work, not reproduced by this paper, numbers taken from original report
